schema_assessment.summary: The schema models a retail/e‑commerce operational dataset with clear separation of orders, order items, products and inventory movements. It is generally well-structured for analytical workloads but lacks explicit primary/foreign key constraints and some referential rigor. With modest cleanup (declaring keys, adding indexes and a few reference tables) it will support reporting, OLAP and NL2SQL queries effectively.

llm_analysis.schema_description: This appears to be a retail or e‑commerce / wholesale domain dataset capturing customers, stores, orders, order items, products, inventory movements, promotions and suppliers. Key entities: customers (customer master and segmentation), orders (transactional sales headers), order_items (sales line items/fact), products (catalog), inventory_transactions (stock movements), promotions (discount rules), stores (physical/virtual locations) and suppliers (vendor master). The model is largely star-like for analytics: orders and inventory_transactions act as fact-ish tables linked to dimensional entities such as customers, products, stores and suppliers. Typical business analyses enabled include sales by customer segment or geography, product/category performance, promotion effectiveness, inventory movement and stock reconciliation, and supplier performance. Top 5 business questions that can be answered: 1) What are sales (revenue, units) by product, category, store and customer segment over time? 2) Which promotions generated the highest lift in orders or revenue and what was their ROI? 3) Which products have the highest inventory turnover or are at risk of stockouts? 4) Who are the top customers by lifetime value and what segments show churn or growth? 5) How do suppliers perform on volume, country, and lead time metrics and which suppliers drive the most cost or quality issues?

table_relationships_overview.relationship_summary: Analytical joins center on orders -> order_items (order_id), and order_items/inventory_transactions -> products (product_id). Orders link to customers (customer_id) and optionally promotions (promotion_id); stores reference customers (store owner/relationship) and can be joined to orders if order-store linkage exists in data. Products reference suppliers (supplier_id) for vendor analytics. inventory_transactions.reference_id/reference_type appears to link stock movements to orders, order items or stores (polymorphic reference) which supports audit and stock reconciliation but should be formalized. These paths enable time series sales and inventory analysis, customer segmentation and cohorting, promotion lift and supplier performance reporting. To improve clarity and analytic performance, add primary/foreign keys, indexes on join columns and normalize any repeated categorical reference columns (e.g., promotion applicable categories).

customers.purpose: Master customer reference for the business: contains customer identifiers, contact and address details, classification (type/segment/status), registration date and credit limit. Serves as the primary source for customer-level reporting, segmentation and as the join point for transactional tables (orders, order_items, promotions, etc.).
customers.key_characteristics: 10,000 rows. Many high-cardinality identifier/contact columns (customer_id, contact_person, email, phone, postal_code, address). Several low-cardinality categorical columns useful for grouping (customer_type: 2 values, customer_segment: 4 values, status: 2 values, country: 1 value). company_name and credit_limit contain ~70% NULLs (company_name likely null for B2C). registration_date stored as nvarchar but has many distinct values (~1,094) and appears to be ISO date strings. credit_limit stored as nvarchar but numeric-like values and high cardinality (~2,992 distinct).
customers.analysis_capabilities: Standalone analysis: customer counts and status (active/inactive), segmentation by type/segment/city/state, geographic distribution, registration trends (after parsing dates), credit exposure distribution (after casting), identification of business customers (company_name non-null) vs consumers, preparing cohorts for retention/churn analysis. Can be joined with orders to analyze revenue, with order_items/products for product affinity by customer, and with promotions to analyze campaign uptake.
business_questions: How many active customers are in each customer_segment and customer_type?
How many new customers registered in a given period (month/quarter/year)?
What is the distribution of customers by city/state and which locations have the most customers?
What is the total and average credit_limit by segment/type (requires casting credit_limit to numeric)?
Which customers (by customer_id or company_name) are inactive or have no recent registrations and should be targeted for reactivation?
customers.relationships: No declared foreign keys, but logical joins: customers.customer_id -> orders.customer_id (orders table) to connect to transactional data (orders, order_items, products). company_name/contact_person/email/phone can be used to match against B2B records in other systems. Joins to promotions likely via orders or a promotion-redemption table. Enables analytics that combine customer attributes with transactions, inventory and product data (inventory_transactions, order_items, orders, products, promotions, stores, suppliers).

inventory_transactions.purpose: Records of inventory movements (inbound and outbound) per product. Each row represents one transaction affecting stock levels and references an origin (Order or Initial Stock). It is the primary event log for inventory flow in the schema.
inventory_transactions.key_characteristics: 177,723 rows; many text columns stored as nvarchar despite numeric/date semantics. transaction_id is effectively unique (177,723 distinct) but no declared primary key. product_id has high cardinality (~994 distinct). transaction_type and reference_type are low-cardinality (2 values each) and heavily skewed: transaction_type is ~99.4% 'OUT' and 0.6% 'IN'; reference_type is ~99.4% 'Order' and 0.6% 'Initial Stock'. quantity values are stored as text and show negative numbers as top values (-1 to -5 and -3 highest). transaction_date is stored as text and has many distinct values (15,649), likely timestamps. reference_id has small null rate (~0.6%) and high cardinality (~15,000 distinct). notes is free text with many distinct values.
inventory_transactions.analysis_capabilities: Can compute transaction counts and totals by product, type, date ranges, and reference. Can derive flows (in vs out) and cumulative stock if initial stock transactions are present and all transactions are included. Can identify high-volume products, peak transaction periods, and trace transactions back to orders via reference_id/reference_type. Cannot guarantee accurate numeric/date arithmetic without data cleaning (quantity and transaction_date require casting/normalization).
business_questions: Which products have the highest outbound (OUT) quantity over a given time range?
What is the daily/weekly trend of inventory movements (IN vs OUT) for a product or product group?
What was the cumulative stock change per product since initial stock entries?
Which orders (reference_id) generated the largest inventory outflows?
Which products have unexpected negative/positive quantity sign patterns relative to transaction_type?
inventory_transactions.relationships: product_id links to products.product_id to enrich item attributes (name, category, cost). reference_id combined with reference_type links to orders (for 'Order') or to an initial stock source (for 'Initial Stock') enabling reconciliation of inventory to order records. Joins to order_items/orders enable order-level analysis (e.g., which order lines caused inventory movements). No explicit foreign keys exist in the DDL; relationships are logical and require schema-level joins on text keys. Useful joins: inventory_transactions.product_id -> products.product_id, inventory_transactions.reference_id -> orders.order_id (when reference_type = 'Order'), and possible joins to suppliers/stores depending on notes/reference conventions.

order_items.purpose: Line-level order data: each row is a single item on an order. It records which product was sold, the quantity and per-item price, and monetary adjustments (discount, tax) and the computed total for that line. This table is the detailed transactional layer that supports roll-ups to orders, products, customers and store-level sales analyses.
order_items.key_characteristics: 176,729 rows. All columns are stored as nvarchar (strings) though many represent numeric values. No declared primary or foreign keys despite high-cardinality order_item_id (176,729 distinct = likely unique) and order_id (15,000 distinct). product_id is moderately high-cardinality (994). quantity, unit_price, discount_amount, tax_amount and total_amount are numeric-looking but stored as text and contain many distinct values (discount/tax/total are almost row-unique). Common quantity values are small integers (1-5). Data likely needs cleaning/casting before numeric aggregations. Average items per order ≈ 176,729 / 15,000 ≈ 11.8.
order_items.analysis_capabilities: Once numeric columns are cast, supports: line-level revenue and margin analysis, per-order aggregation (order totals, items per order), per-product sales, basket analysis (most common quantities/combos), discount and tax impact studies, time-series if joined to orders table with date, inventory impact when joined to products/inventory tables, and anomaly detection on totals vs computed (quantity * unit_price - discount + tax).
business_questions: What is total revenue, total discounts and tax collected over a time period (after joining to orders for dates)?
Which products generate the most revenue and the highest quantity sold?
What is the average number of items and average line total per order?
How do discounts and taxes affect line totals and margins (e.g., average discount per line or percent discount)?
Are there mismatches between reported total_amount and computed line total (quantity*unit_price - discount + tax)?
order_items.relationships: order_id links each line to an orders table (enables order-level aggregations, customer and date context, store info). product_id links to products (enables product attributes, category roll-ups, supplier linkage). Joining to inventory_transactions supports stock movement and reconciliation; promotions can be joined (if promotion mapping exists) to analyze discount sources; stores/customers suppliers become available via orders->stores/customers and products->suppliers. These joins allow answers like revenue by store, customer lifetime value, supplier contribution, inventory depletion and promotion effectiveness.

orders.purpose: This table records individual customer orders (transaction header) including identifiers, monetary amounts, status, payment and shipping metadata. It functions as the central sales/orders source in the schema and is the primary join point to order_items, customers and promotions for revenue, fulfillment and customer analytics.
orders.key_characteristics: Many columns are stored as nvarchar (strings) although they represent dates or numeric monetary values — this requires casting/normalization before numeric/date analysis. High-cardinality columns: order_id, customer_id, order_date, subtotal_amount, tax_amount, final_amount, shipping_address, delivery_date (many unique values). Low-cardinality categorical fields: order_status (5 values), payment_method (6 values), promotion_id (3 values but 94.5% null). num_items has small numeric range (distinct 50). discount_amount is mostly zero (97.1%). Delivery_date has ~35% nulls. There is no declared primary key or foreign keys in the DDL despite logical keys implied by column names.
orders.analysis_capabilities: Alone, this table supports order-level analyses: order counts and revenue/average order value over time, payment method distribution, order status distribution, discount usage and impact on revenue, basic delivery performance (order_date -> delivery_date where available), shipping cost distribution, and identifying high-value customers (by customer_id aggregated). It cannot provide item-level product breakdowns without joining order_items and products, nor inventory or supplier-level analytics without corresponding joins.
business_questions: What is total revenue and order count by day/week/month?
Which payment methods and order statuses contribute most to revenue?
How often and how much are discounts used, and what is their impact on final_amount?
What is the distribution of shipping charges and which orders pay shipping?
What is on-time delivery performance (where delivery_date exists) and average delivery lag?
orders.relationships: Logical relationships: orders.customer_id -> customers.customer_id (customer-level segmentation/retention). orders.order_id -> order_items.order_id (to get item-level details, product ids, quantities, margins). orders.promotion_id -> promotions.promotion_id (to evaluate promotion effectiveness). Through order_items -> products -> suppliers/inventory_transactions you can analyze product-level sales, supply and inventory impacts. These joins enable multi-dimensional analytics: sales by product, store performance if store information is available via other tables, and promotion-to-sales attribution.

products.purpose: Catalog of products sold by the business. Contains identification (ids, sku, name), classification (category, subcategory, brand), pricing/costs, inventory snapshot, supplier link, physical attributes (weight) and creation date. Serves as the product master for sales, inventory, procurement and promotion analyses.
products.key_characteristics: Most identifier fields (product_id, sku, product_name) are high-cardinality and appear nearly unique (product_id distinct = row count). Several numeric and date attributes are stored as nvarchar (unit_price, cost_price, stock_quantity, weight_kg, created_date) which requires casting/cleaning before arithmetic/time operations. category is low-cardinality (~7 values) and subcategory/brand are moderate. No declared primary key or FKs. Data shows floating point text representations (many long decimal strings) and ISO-like dates (YYYY-MM-DD) stored as text. Some columns contain NULLs.
products.analysis_capabilities: By itself this table can support SKU/product-level reporting: price and cost analysis, margin calculation (after casting), inventory level snapshots, supplier product counts, weight-based shipping or handling calculations, product lifecycle (first seen/created_date distribution), category/subcategory breakdowns, and product counts by brand. It cannot show sales or transaction-level trends without joining order_items/orders/inventory_transactions.
business_questions: Which products have the highest gross margin (unit_price - cost_price) and what are their categories?
How many products are currently low-stock (stock_quantity below a threshold) per supplier or category?
What is the distribution of products by category/subcategory/brand?
When were products added (created_date): how many new products per month/quarter?
Which suppliers provide the most products and which product lines (by category) are concentrated with a single supplier?
products.relationships: Likely joins: suppliers via supplier_id (supplier product attributes and supplier metadata), order_items (to link product master to sales and calculate revenue, requires verifying whether order_items uses product_id or sku), inventory_transactions (to reconcile stock_quantity vs transactions), promotions (to apply product-level promotions), stores/inventory for per-location stock distribution. These relationships enable end-to-end analyses: sales performance, inventory turnover, supplier performance, promotion lift, and forecasting. Because there are no declared FKs, NL2SQL systems should verify actual join keys and may need to join on sku or product_id depending on downstream table columns.

promotions.purpose: Catalog of promotional offers (discounts, BOGO, bundles, cashback) used by the business to apply incentives to orders/products/customers. It defines promotion metadata and constraints (dates, eligibility, caps) that are used when analyzing promotion performance or when enforcing promotion rules.
promotions.key_characteristics: Small table (50 rows) with high-cardinality identifier (promotion_id distinct = row count) and many textual columns storing numeric and date semantics as nvarchar. promotion_type is low-cardinality (5 values). status is highly skewed (94% Expired). Many numeric-like columns have large null fractions (discount_percentage null 68%, discount_amount null 84%, max_discount_amount null 82%, usage_limit_per_customer null 40%). Dates are stored as strings (start_date and end_date distinct ~49). applicable_category small set with some nulls (8%). min_order_amount has limited distinct values and is a good filter field. Many columns are better cast to numeric/date types for analysis.
promotions.analysis_capabilities: Alone this table can be used to: count and profile promotions (by type, category, customer type, status), compute scheduled windows and durations, identify currently active promotions using start/end/status, summarize constraint distributions (min order amounts, caps, per-customer limits), and detect configuration issues (overlapping promotions, missing numeric/date types). It cannot measure promotion effectiveness (redemptions, revenue lift) without joining to orders/order_items/customers/products tables.
business_questions: How many promotions exist by promotion_type and applicable_category?
Which promotions are currently active or will start within a given date range?
What are the typical discount values and caps (percent vs amount) across promotions?
Which promotions are restricted to B2B or B2C, and how many promos target each customer type?
Which promotions have low usage limits (per-customer or total) that may constrain adoption?
promotions.relationships: No declared foreign keys in DDL. Logical joins commonly used: join to orders or order_items on promotion_id (if order data records promotion_id) to measure redemptions; join applicable_category to products.category to restrict product-level analysis; use applicable_customer_type to filter joins to customers (customers table) or orders by customer type; join start/end date ranges to order/order_items timestamps to find orders that occurred during promotion windows. Because fields are strings, consistent normalization (casting) is required before joins and date-range comparisons.

stores.purpose: This table stores master-level information about retail stores (locations) for the business: identifiers, ownership customer, descriptive attributes (name, type), address and geography, opening date, physical size and employee counts. It acts as the canonical store dimension for analyses that require store characteristics and as the join point between transactional tables (orders, inventory, order_items) and store-level attributes.
stores.key_characteristics: 12 columns, 2,500 rows. No declared primary or foreign keys. Many text columns stored as nvarchar (including numeric/date-like values). High-cardinality identifier and address-like columns (store_id: 2500 distinct; address/postal_code/size_sqft also high-cardinality). Low-cardinality categorical column store_type (3 values). Geographic columns have limited distinct values (city: 18, state: 11, country: 1). opening_date is stored as nvarchar but shows ISO-like yyyy-mm-dd patterns — treat as date. size_sqft and employee_count are numeric in meaning but stored as strings and should be cast for numeric aggregations. customer_id appears frequently repeated (1502 distinct) and is a likely FK to customers.
stores.analysis_capabilities: Alone, this table supports: inventory of store attributes, counts of stores by type/city/state, opening-date based cohorting (new vs legacy stores) after casting to date, size and employee distributions (after casting), and basic geospatial rollups (store counts by city/state). It also enables filtering of stores for downstream joins (e.g., only Ecommerce stores). It does not contain sales/transaction metrics — requires joins for revenue/transactions analysis.
business_questions: How many stores do we have by store_type, city and state?
Which stores opened in the last 12 months (or a given date range)?
What is the distribution of store sizes (sqft) and employee_count across store types?
Which customers own the largest number of stores (by customer_id)?
List stores in a given city or postal code area for operations planning
stores.relationships: Likely join keys: customer_id -> customers.customer_id (owner/account), store_id -> orders.store_id or inventory_transactions.store_id or other transaction tables (if those tables reference store). These joins enable analyses linking store characteristics to sales, order performance, inventory levels, promotions effectiveness, and supplier deliveries. Because no FK constraints are declared, joins should be validated (nulls and mismatches).

suppliers.purpose: Master list of external suppliers providing goods or components to the business; holds supplier identity and contact/metadata used for supplier-level analysis, filtering and joining with transactional tables (products, orders, inventory, promotions) to measure supply-side activity and performance.
suppliers.key_characteristics: 100 rows, no declared primary key or foreign keys. supplier_id appears unique (100 distinct) but nullable. Several contact columns (contact_person, email, phone) are effectively unique per row. country has low cardinality (10) with clear concentration in China/France/Italy/Japan/South Korea. rating and established_year are stored as nvarchar despite numeric semantics: rating has 21 distinct string values and contains decimal-like string representations (e.g., '3.10000000000000'), established_year has ~35 distinct values but values are 4-digit years stored as text. supplier_name has moderate cardinality (56) with some repeats. Many columns may contain NULLs. Small dataset (100 rows) — optimization priorities differ from large tables but data type corrections and indexes can improve NL2SQL accuracy and performance.
suppliers.analysis_capabilities: Alone this table supports supplier counts, distribution by country, rating distributions, supplier age (via established_year), completeness of contact information, and supplier metadata enrichment for joins. You can identify top-rated suppliers, suppliers by region, suppliers established before/after a year, and prepare joins to products/orders for supplier-level transactional analysis when linked by supplier_id or supplier_name.
business_questions: How many suppliers do we have and how are they distributed by country?
Which suppliers have the highest/lowest ratings?
Which suppliers were established before a given year (e.g., 1990)?
Which suppliers are missing contact information (email or phone)?
What is the average rating per country or per cohort of established_year?
suppliers.relationships: No explicit FKs declared, but typical relationships in the schema: products or order_items often contain supplier_id and can be joined to this table to analyze supplier product portfolios, purchase volumes, order history and inventory transactions. Joining suppliers to orders/order_items/products enables supplier-level revenue, return and lead-time analysis. Because supplier_id is textual and not enforced, joins must defensively handle duplicates and NULLs and rely on consistent formatting (trim, case) or fallback to supplier_name if needed.

customer_id.description: Unique customer identifier string (nvarchar). Appears to be unique for each row (10,000 distinct values).
customer_id.analysis_use: Primary lookup key for joins to transactional tables (orders). Useful for deduplication, per-customer aggregations (orders count, revenue), and detail drills. High-cardinality column for filters and exact-match queries.
business_questions: Which customers placed the most orders (requires join to orders)?
Show customer details for a given customer_id for support or reconciliation.
customer_id.nl2sql_hints: Treat as the primary join key to orders. Use exact equality predicates (WHERE customer_id = 'CUST0000...') rather than LIKE. Consider creating an index and/or primary key if logical uniqueness must be enforced. Value is nvarchar and length varies (max observed 11); preserve type but cast only when necessary.

customer_type.description: High-level type classification: B2C or B2B.
customer_type.analysis_use: Low-cardinality grouping/filtering for performance-friendly aggregations (e.g., counts, averages). Useful to split metrics (orders, credit) by type.
business_questions: What percentage of customers are B2B vs B2C?
How does average order value differ between B2B and B2C customers?
customer_type.nl2sql_hints: Exact-match filters (customer_type = 'B2B' or 'B2C'). Use in GROUP BY. Because it's low-cardinality, predicates here are selective only when combined with other filters. Values are textual; watch for whitespace/case differences.

customer_segment.description: More granular customer segmentation (Direct Consumer, Modern Trade, Traditional Trade, Ecommerce).
customer_segment.analysis_use: Primary grouping dimension for segmentation, cohorting, and performance comparisons (counts, credit exposure, revenue when joined). Good for group-by aggregations and segment-level dashboards.
business_questions: Which segments drive the most revenue (join to orders)?
How many active customers are in each segment?
customer_segment.nl2sql_hints: Use for GROUP BY and filters. Low cardinality (4 values) makes it efficient for summarization. Normalize spelling/case if queries are free-text sensitive.

company_name.description: Business/company name for B2B customers; ~70% NULL (likely null for individual consumers).
company_name.analysis_use: Identify business accounts, deduplicate corporate customers, display B2B customer lists and aggregate metrics at company level (e.g., total orders per company). NULL presence is informative for B2C detection.
business_questions: List top companies by total orders or revenue (requires join to orders).
Which companies have credit_limit assigned and what are their credit exposures?
company_name.nl2sql_hints: Expect many NULLs — use IS NULL / IS NOT NULL to distinguish B2C vs B2B. For NL2SQL, account for possible partial name matches (use LIKE) and duplicates; company_name is not a reliable unique join key. Trim/case-fold when matching user-provided text.

contact_person.description: Name of the contact person at the customer/company. High cardinality (likely unique per customer).
contact_person.analysis_use: Contact-level reporting, de-duplicating contacts across companies, support lookup and targeted communications. Rarely used for grouping due to high cardinality; used for display and filter by name.
business_questions: Retrieve contact details for customer support for a given customer/company.
Which contacts belong to top X customers by revenue?
contact_person.nl2sql_hints: High-cardinality textual field; prefer exact-match or prefix matching for lookups. Be careful with free-text queries: normalize spaces/case. Not suitable as join key for transactional tables.

email.description: Customer email address; high cardinality and likely unique per customer.
email.analysis_use: Contact channel analysis, deduplication, campaign targeting, and as a secondary identifier for lookups. Useful for WHERE email = '...' or LIKE '%@domain.com' (campaign counts).
business_questions: How many customers have corporate email domains vs consumer domains?
Which active customers do not have an email on file?
email.nl2sql_hints: Treat as string; exact-match and domain-pattern matches are common. Watch for NULLs and malformed addresses. Use LOWER() when matching domains in NL2SQL to handle case-sensitivity.

phone.description: Customer phone number; high cardinality, stored as text with formatting characters.
phone.analysis_use: Contactability analysis and support lookups. Can be used for normalization and de-duplication (after stripping non-digits).
business_questions: How many customers lack phone numbers?
List customers with phone numbers from a particular country code.
phone.nl2sql_hints: Phone numbers include formatting: for reliable filtering normalize by removing non-digits (REGEXP/REPLACE) and use prefix matching for country codes. Avoid numeric casting because of leading '+' and separators.

address.description: Street address free-text field; high cardinality and many unique values.
address.analysis_use: Display and geocoding, deduplication at household or business location level. Not ideal for grouping due to uniqueness; useful for proximity analysis after geocoding.
business_questions: Which customers are located at the same address (possible duplicates)?
How many customers live in a given neighborhood or street (after geocoding)?
address.nl2sql_hints: Free-text matching requires normalization (trim, upper/lower, remove punctuation). For NL2SQL, prefer searches like WHERE address LIKE '%Elm Blvd%' and avoid equality lookups unless normalized.

city.description: City of the customer; low-to-moderate cardinality (18 distinct values).
city.analysis_use: Geographic grouping and filtering for regional reporting, join to store catchment areas, heatmaps and city-level KPIs.
business_questions: Which cities have the highest number of active customers?
How does average credit_limit vary by city?
city.nl2sql_hints: Use for GROUP BY city. Watch for inconsistent naming (e.g., 'New York' vs 'NYC'); consider normalization. Suitable for use in WHERE clauses (city = 'New York').

state.description: Two-letter state code (observed lengths up to 2); 11 distinct states with distribution across the dataset.
state.analysis_use: Important region-level grouping and filtering. Good for state-level KPIs and selecting customers in specific operational jurisdictions.
business_questions: How many active customers are in CA versus other states?
Which states have the highest average credit_limit?
state.nl2sql_hints: Low cardinality; ideal for GROUP BY and filters. Expect standard two-letter codes—use equality predicates. Combine with city/postal_code for finer geographic filters.

country.description: Country code/text; single value 'USA' for all rows.
country.analysis_use: Not useful as a differentiator in this dataset (single value) but kept for compatibility with multi-country schemas.
business_questions: Are all customers in the USA?
Filter customers by country when integrating with multi-country sources (future-proofing).
country.nl2sql_hints: Single-value column—can be ignored for filtering in current dataset. NL2SQL parsers can treat as static value; avoid unnecessary predicates on country unless explicitly requested.

postal_code.description: Postal (ZIP) code as text; high cardinality (~9,458 distinct) and stored as nvarchar length ~5.
postal_code.analysis_use: Granular geographic analysis, mapping, and linking to demographic datasets. Good for region-level aggregations and segmentation by ZIP.
business_questions: Which ZIP codes have the highest customer density?
List customers in a specific postal code or set of postal codes.
postal_code.nl2sql_hints: Stored as text—use string equality or prefix matches for 5-digit patterns. Normalize leading/trailing whitespace. For range or proximity queries, map to lat/long externally; avoid numeric casting.

registration_date.description: Customer registration date stored as nvarchar (ISO-like strings observed, e.g., '2025-04-01').
registration_date.analysis_use: Critical for cohort and trend analysis (new customers over time). Must be cast to DATE or DATETIME for date arithmetic, grouping by month/quarter/year, and filtering by ranges.
business_questions: How many customers registered each month in the last 12 months?
What is the cohort retention for customers registered in a given quarter?
registration_date.nl2sql_hints: Must CAST/CONVERT to DATE/TIMESTAMP in NL2SQL-generated queries (e.g., CAST(registration_date AS DATE) or TRY_CAST for safety). Validate date format; handle invalid/missing values. Use date functions for grouping (MONTH/DATE_TRUNC). For performance, consider persisting a parsed date column or creating a computed column/index.

status.description: Customer operational status: Active or Inactive (93.9% Active).
status.analysis_use: Primary filter for active-customer metrics and eligibility checks (e.g., promotions). Useful for retention, churn analysis and operational dashboards.
business_questions: How many active vs inactive customers are there by segment?
Which inactive customers were registered within the last year?
status.nl2sql_hints: Exact-match categorical filter (status = 'Active'). Because of skew (most values Active), combine with other predicates for selective queries. Normalize case and whitespace in NL2SQL mappings.

credit_limit.description: Customer credit limit stored as nvarchar though values are numeric-like; ~70% NULL and ~2,992 distinct values.
credit_limit.analysis_use: Financial exposure and risk analysis (sum/avg/median by segment/type). Requires casting to numeric (DECIMAL/FLOAT) for aggregations; handle NULLs carefully. Useful to identify customers with high credit lines.
business_questions: What is the total and average credit_limit for active B2B customers?
Which customers exceed a specified credit limit threshold?
credit_limit.nl2sql_hints: Always CAST or TRY_CAST to numeric before aggregation (e.g., TRY_CAST(credit_limit AS DECIMAL(18,2))). Handle NULLs and non-numeric strings with TRY_CAST/ISNUMERIC checks. Because of many NULLs, use COALESCE for default values if needed. For performance and correctness, consider normalizing to a numeric column and indexing if frequently filtered/aggregated.

transaction_id.description: Unique identifier for each inventory transaction event.
transaction_id.analysis_use: Row-level identity used for deduplication, last-event selection, and as a stable identifier when joining to other logs. Useful for counting distinct transactions and ensuring idempotency.
business_questions: How many distinct inventory transactions occurred in a period?
What are the details of a specific transaction_id for audit or investigation?
transaction_id.nl2sql_hints: Treat as a string key. Expect near-unique cardinality (distinct = row count). Use for exact equality filters (WHERE transaction_id = '...') and deduplication (SELECT DISTINCT). Consider adding an index/PK on transaction_id for performance. Do not attempt to CAST to numeric.

product_id.description: Identifier of the product affected by the transaction.
product_id.analysis_use: Primary grouping/join key for product-level aggregations (SUM quantities, count transactions), joins to products table to get descriptions/categories, and filters for SKU-level reports.
business_questions: Which products have the highest net stock outflows over the last month?
How many transactions occurred for a specific product in a time window?
product_id.nl2sql_hints: Treat as text; join to products on exact equality (inventory_transactions.product_id = products.product_id). Consider normalizing case/whitespace. Because cardinality is high, use indexed product_id for faster GROUP BY and joins. When filtering by multiple products, use IN (..).

transaction_type.description: Categorical indicator of movement direction, e.g., 'IN' or 'OUT'.
transaction_type.analysis_use: Filter/group transactions into inbound vs outbound; used to interpret quantity sign and to segment trends and reports.
business_questions: What is the split of inbound vs outbound transactions by count and quantity?
How do IN transactions (receipts/initial stock) impact cumulative stock compared to OUT transactions (sales/shipments)?
transaction_type.nl2sql_hints: Low-cardinality string column. Use exact matches (WHERE transaction_type = 'OUT'). Be careful: quantity values appear negative for OUT; verify sign convention before summing. Consider normalizing values (e.g., UPPER(TRIM(transaction_type))). Use as an efficient filter when indexed.

quantity.description: The amount of stock moved in the transaction, stored as text (nvarchar). Values appear numeric and often negative (e.g., -1, -3).
quantity.analysis_use: Primary numeric measure for aggregations (SUM, AVG) after casting to integer/decimal. Used to calculate net movements and cumulative balances. Can also be used for anomaly detection (non-numeric entries).
business_questions: What is the total quantity moved (sum) for a product or period?
Are there transactions with unexpected quantities (e.g., zero, extremely large, or non-numeric)?
quantity.nl2sql_hints: Must CAST/CONVERT to numeric type in queries (e.g., CAST(NULLIF(LTRIM(RTRIM(quantity)), '') AS INT)) and handle non-numeric or null values with TRY_CAST/TRY_CONVERT where supported. Confirm whether negative values correspond to OUT and positive to IN — the data shows OUT transactions are often negative; avoid double-negating. Use COALESCE to handle nulls. When grouping by transaction_type, ensure consistency between sign and type.

transaction_date.description: Timestamp of when the transaction occurred stored as nvarchar (text). Likely in 'YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS' or similar format (max len 19).
transaction_date.analysis_use: Primary dimension for time-series analysis (daily/weekly/monthly aggregations), ordering, and filtering by date ranges. Useful for windowed cumulative calculations.
business_questions: What are the daily counts and sums of inventory transactions?
When did a specific product experience the largest net stock change?
transaction_date.nl2sql_hints: Parse to a proper datetime (e.g., CAST(transaction_date AS DATETIME) or TRY_CAST). Normalize formats and timezones if needed. When writing NL2SQL, expect date filters like 'last 30 days' and translate them to parsed date comparisons. Consider adding a computed/date column of proper type for performance and partitioning.

reference_id.description: Identifier for the source or related entity of the transaction (e.g., order id). Some values may be null (~0.6%).
reference_id.analysis_use: Join key to orders or other reference tables when reference_type indicates 'Order'; used for tracing transactions back to business documents and for aggregating by order.
business_questions: Which orders correspond to the largest outbound inventory movements?
For a given order (reference_id), what inventory transactions were created?
reference_id.nl2sql_hints: Treat as text and use in conditional joins when reference_type = 'Order'. Use equality joins (inventory_transactions.reference_id = orders.order_id). Handle nulls (WHERE reference_id IS NOT NULL) and possible mismatch in formatting (trim/uppercase). Consider filtering by reference_type before joining to avoid incorrect matches.

reference_type.analysis_use: Filter or group transactions by business origin. Key to identify initial stock records vs transactional movements tied to orders.
business_questions: How much of current stock movement originates from initial stock records versus orders?
How many transactions are initial stock adjustments compared to order-driven movements?
reference_type.nl2sql_hints: Low-cardinality string. Use in WHERE clauses to route joins (e.g., WHEN reference_type = 'Order' JOIN orders). Normalize values (TRIM/UPPER) and beware of minor variations. Use as a pre-filter for logic branching in NL2SQL generation.

notes.description: Free-text notes about the transaction (reason, context).
notes.analysis_use: Qualitative field useful for troubleshooting, extracting keywords (e.g., store or supplier codes), and for ad-hoc filters (LIKE '%return%'). Can be used for anomaly detection and enrichment via text parsing.
business_questions: Which transactions are marked as returns or adjustments according to notes?
Are there patterns in notes that correspond to specific inventory issues or suppliers?
notes.nl2sql_hints: Text field—use LIKE/ILIKE or full-text search where available. Expect varied content and require safe escaping for special characters. Avoid using notes for joins unless cleaned/mapped. When generating NL2SQL, allow users to query by keywords and convert to WHERE notes LIKE '%keyword%'.

order_item_id.description: Unique identifier for the line item (string). Distinct count equals row count, suggesting it functions as a row-level unique id.
order_item_id.analysis_use: Primary candidate for de-duplicating rows, auditing and row-level joins/updates. Useful for debugging, tracing anomalies and building a true primary key. Not typically used for aggregation other than counts.
business_questions: Can we identify and remove duplicate/phantom line items by order_item_id?
Which specific line items are anomalous in total_amount vs computed value?
order_item_id.nl2sql_hints: Treat as string key in joins. Consider enforcing uniqueness and NOT NULL in ETL. When building NL2SQL responses, use order_item_id for single-record fetches (WHERE order_item_id = '...'). If you intend to set it as PK, confirm all values non-null and unique first.

order_id.description: Identifier for the order that this line belongs to (string). 15,000 distinct values across 176,729 rows — multiple items per order.
order_id.analysis_use: Key for grouping to order-level metrics (items per order, order totals) and joining to orders/customers/stores for contextual dimensions (order date, customer_id, store_id). Frequent filter for time ranges via joined orders table.
business_questions: What is the total value and number of items for a specific order?
How many lines (items) does an average order contain?
order_id.nl2sql_hints: Use this as a join key to seed_data_raw.orders. Keep as string in SQL predicates unless normalized. For numeric aggregation of order totals, SUM(CAST(total_amount AS DECIMAL(...))) GROUP BY order_id. Consider indexing order_id for faster GROUP BY and joins.

product_id.description: Identifier of the product sold for this line (string). ~994 distinct product values; top products show small percentage shares.
product_id.analysis_use: Join to products for name/category/brand; group by product_id to get units sold, revenue, average price; identify top sellers and underperformers.
business_questions: Which products have the highest revenue and highest quantities sold?
What is the average unit_price by product and how consistent is pricing (price variance)?
Which products received the most discount (sum discount_amount)?
product_id.nl2sql_hints: Use as join key to seed_data_raw.products. Product_id is string—ensure exact matching (trim whitespace). When ranking top products, cast monetary fields and aggregate: SUM(CAST(total_amount AS DECIMAL(18,4))) GROUP BY product_id. Consider indexing product_id for frequent product-level queries.

quantity.description: Quantity of the product sold on this line (stored as nvarchar). Small integer values dominate; distinct ~496 (may include formatting noise or junk).
quantity.analysis_use: Aggregate to compute units sold per product/order/store; used for computing revenue/quantity relationships and average basket size. Also used in validation of total_amount computations.
business_questions: How many units of a product were sold in a period?
What is the distribution of quantities per line (single-item purchases vs multi-item)?
Which orders include large-quantity lines that may indicate bulk purchases or data errors?
quantity.nl2sql_hints: Treat as integer after cleaning. Use TRY_CAST or equivalent: TRY_CAST(NULLIF(TRIM(quantity), '') AS INT) or CAST(REPLACE(quantity, ',', '') AS INT) depending on possible formatting. Guard against non-numeric strings; handle NULL/empty as 0 or exclude depending on business rule.

unit_price.description: Price per unit for the product on this line (stored as nvarchar). Many distinct values, decimals visible in top values.
unit_price.analysis_use: Compute line revenue (quantity * unit_price), price analytics (average price, price dispersion), and detect pricing anomalies or promotions. Used to recompute/validate total_amount.
business_questions: What is the average and median unit price by product or category?
Are there price anomalies (extremely high or low unit_price) requiring investigation?
How often does unit_price change for the same product?
unit_price.nl2sql_hints: Must be cast to DECIMAL for math: TRY_CAST(REPLACE(unit_price, ',', '') AS DECIMAL(18,4)). Watch for localization (commas for thousands) and currency symbols. For NL2SQL, recommend rewriting numeric expressions explicitly: SUM(CAST(quantity AS INT) * CAST(unit_price AS DECIMAL(18,4))). Consider creating a persisted numeric column in ETL for performance.

discount_amount.description: Discount applied to the line (string representing numeric currency). Very high distinct count indicating line-specific discounts.
discount_amount.analysis_use: Aggregate to measure total discounts, compute net revenue, analyze discount frequency and size, and correlate discounts with sales uplift by product/promo.
business_questions: How much discount was given in total and by product/store?
What is the average discount per line and as a percent of unit_price?
Which orders or products receive the highest total discount amounts?
discount_amount.nl2sql_hints: Cast to DECIMAL: TRY_CAST(NULLIF(REPLACE(discount_amount, ',', ''), '') AS DECIMAL(18,4)). Beware of negative values or credit-related entries. When computing percent discount: (CAST(discount_amount AS DECIMAL)/ (CAST(unit_price AS DECIMAL)*CAST(quantity AS INT))) * 100, guard division by zero. Many rows may have zero or null—handle explicitly.

tax_amount.description: Tax applied to the line (string representing numeric currency). High distinctness, likely many small variations per line.
tax_amount.analysis_use: Aggregate total tax collected, analyze tax by jurisdiction when joined to orders/stores, and validate tax computation relative to taxable amounts.
business_questions: How much tax was collected over a time period or by store?
Is tax being computed correctly relative to unit_price and applicable rates?
tax_amount.nl2sql_hints: Cast to DECIMAL for aggregation: TRY_CAST(REPLACE(tax_amount, ',', '') AS DECIMAL(18,4)). When validating totals, compare tax_amount to expected tax = taxable_base * rate; if rate depends on store/jurisdiction, join to orders/stores. Handle NULL/empty as zero if appropriate.

total_amount.description: Reported line total (string). Should represent final line charge: quantity*unit_price minus discount plus tax (but must be validated). Distinct values nearly as many as rows, indicating mostly unique totals.
total_amount.analysis_use: Primary measure for revenue when summing at line-level. Used to validate arithmetic: total_amount ?= quantity * unit_price - discount + tax. Aggregate for total revenue, returns analysis, and reconciliations against accounting.
business_questions: What is the total revenue (sum of total_amount) for a period, store or product?
Which line items have mismatches between recorded total_amount and recomputed total?
total_amount.nl2sql_hints: Always CAST to DECIMAL before aggregation: SUM(TRY_CAST(REPLACE(total_amount, ',', '') AS DECIMAL(18,4)))) or SUM(CAST(... AS DECIMAL(18,4))). For validation, compare with computed expression using appropriate casts and rounding tolerances: ABS(CAST(total_amount AS DECIMAL(18,4)) - (CAST(quantity AS INT)*CAST(unit_price AS DECIMAL(18,4)) - CAST(discount_amount AS DECIMAL(18,4)) + CAST(tax_amount AS DECIMAL(18,4)))) < 0.01. Because it's stored as text, NL2SQL generators should produce casts and safe parsing logic, handle NULL/empty, and warn if any non-numeric tokens exist.

order_id.description: Unique identifier for the order (transaction header). Stored as nvarchar; max observed length 11. No declared primary key.
order_id.analysis_use: Primary grouping/identifier for order-level aggregations; join key to order_items and other order-level tables. Use for deduplication and auditing.
business_questions: How many unique orders were placed in a given period?
Which orders correspond to returned or cancelled transactions?
order_id.nl2sql_hints: Treat as string key when joining to order_items; ensure no invisible duplicates (trim/normalize). Consider casting or enforcing uniqueness when designing queries. Use equality joins (ORDER_ID = ...) and avoid numeric casts.

customer_id.description: Identifier for the customer who placed the order. nvarchar; high cardinality (~7.5k distinct).
customer_id.analysis_use: Group and aggregate orders by customer for RFM, lifetime value, repeat purchase rates, and join to customers table for demographics.
business_questions: Who are the top customers by revenue or order count?
What is the repeat purchase rate per customer cohort?
customer_id.nl2sql_hints: Join to customers on exact string match. Cast/trim if customer_id formatting inconsistent. High-cardinality—avoid unnecessary GROUP BY on customer_id unless doing targeted customer-level reports.

order_date.description: Order placement date/time stored as nvarchar (observed max length 19). Many distinct values (~14996) indicating timestamp granularity.
order_date.analysis_use: Primary time dimension for trend analyses (orders/day/week/month), cohorting and delivery lag calculations (when paired with delivery_date). Requires parsing/casting to date/datetime in queries.
business_questions: How do orders trend over time (daily/weekly/monthly)?
What is the conversion of orders per marketing campaign period?
order_date.nl2sql_hints: Convert string to DATE or DATETIME explicitly (e.g., CAST/CONVERT). Normalize time zones if relevant. Provide multiple granularities (date, week, month) in NL2SQL generation. Handle malformed/null strings robustly.

order_status.description: Order lifecycle status (Categorical: Delivered, Shipped, Confirmed, Pending, Cancelled).
order_status.analysis_use: Filter and group by status to compute fulfillment rates, cancellation rates, revenue from non-cancelled orders, and backlog (Pending).
business_questions: What percentage of orders are delivered vs cancelled?
How much revenue is tied up in non-delivered statuses (Pending/Shipped)?
order_status.nl2sql_hints: Treat as categorical string; consider normalizing capitalization/whitespace. Use WHERE order_status = 'Delivered' etc. When generating queries, include status filters for revenue calculations (exclude Cancelled).

num_items.description: Number of items in the order stored as nvarchar (small integer-like values, distinct 50).
num_items.analysis_use: Aggregation (average items per order), histogram/buckets for order sizing, segmentation (small vs large orders). Cross-check against SUM of quantities from order_items for data quality.
business_questions: What is the average number of items per order?
How does average order value vary with number of items?
num_items.nl2sql_hints: Cast to integer in queries (e.g., CAST(num_items AS INT)). Watch for non-numeric values or nulls. For grouping, consider bucketing (1,2-3,4-6,7+).

subtotal_amount.description: Order subtotal before discounts, tax and shipping stored as nvarchar (monetary values). Very high cardinality.
subtotal_amount.analysis_use: Summation for revenue reconciliation and calculation of discounts/taxes; typically used as base for computing margins and price analyses.
business_questions: What is total subtotal (pre-discount) revenue over the period?
Which orders have unusually high or low subtotals?
subtotal_amount.nl2sql_hints: Cast to numeric/decimal explicitly (e.g., CAST(subtotal_amount AS DECIMAL(18,2))). Handle currency and thousands separators if present. Use COALESCE/NULL handling for malformed entries.

discount_amount.description: Discount applied to the order stored as nvarchar. Mostly zero (97.1%); 2.7% nulls; 130 distinct values.
discount_amount.analysis_use: Measure discount frequency, total discount given, average discount per order and impact on final_amount/revenue. Useful for promotion effectiveness when joined to promotion_id.
business_questions: How much discount was applied in total and per order on average?
Which promotions or order segments receive the most discounts?
discount_amount.nl2sql_hints: Cast to numeric. Recognize that many zeros indicate sparsity — use filters discount_amount > 0 to find promoted orders. Treat nulls as zero only if business rules allow (otherwise explicitly handle null). Join to promotions when promotion_id present.

tax_amount.description: Tax applied to the order stored as nvarchar; high cardinality and 2.7% nulls.
tax_amount.analysis_use: Aggregate tax liability, compute effective tax rates (tax_amount / taxable subtotal), and validate final_amount arithmetic.
business_questions: What is total tax collected by period or region?
What is the average tax rate applied across orders?
tax_amount.nl2sql_hints: Cast to numeric. Be careful with nulls and rounding. Consider computing tax rate only when subtotal_amount > 0 and tax_amount is not null.

shipping_amount.description: Shipping charge for the order stored as nvarchar; many zeroes (70.4%) indicating free shipping for most orders; otherwise many distinct values.
shipping_amount.analysis_use: Analyze shipping revenue/costs, identify orders incurring shipping, and study correlation between shipping pricing and order size/value.
business_questions: What percentage of orders pay for shipping and what is average shipping fee?
Does charging shipping reduce order conversion or average order value?
shipping_amount.nl2sql_hints: Cast to numeric. Treat '0.0' as free shipping. Use filters shipping_amount > 0 to analyze paying orders. Watch for precision/representation artifacts in string form (e.g., trailing zeros).

final_amount.description: Final charged amount after discounts, tax and shipping stored as nvarchar; high cardinality and 2.7% nulls.
final_amount.analysis_use: Primary revenue measure for aggregation (sum, average), compute AOV (average order value), and reconcile with subtotal/discount/tax/shipping.
business_questions: What is total final revenue by period and by customer segment?
How does final_amount compare to subtotal (what is average discount/tax/shipping impact)?
final_amount.nl2sql_hints: Cast to numeric before aggregation. When generating NL2SQL, prefer final_amount as revenue metric but validate arithmetic: final_amount ≈ subtotal_amount - discount_amount + tax_amount + shipping_amount (use CASTs). Handle null final_amounts explicitly.

payment_method.description: Payment instrument used (Wire Transfer, Credit Card, Net Banking, UPI, Debit Card, etc.). Low cardinality (~6 values).
payment_method.analysis_use: Segment revenue by payment method, compute payment-method specific conversion or refund rates, and measure average order amounts per payment channel.
business_questions: Which payment methods generate the most revenue and orders?
Are certain payment methods associated with higher cancellation or refund rates?
payment_method.nl2sql_hints: Treat as categorical string; normalize values. Use GROUP BY payment_method for distribution. When translating NL to SQL, allow synonyms (e.g., 'card' -> 'Credit Card'/'Debit Card') and map common abbreviations.

shipping_address.description: Full shipping address text (nvarchar). High cardinality (~7519 distinct) and likely contains city/state/zip components in free text.
shipping_address.analysis_use: Geographic grouping after parsing (city/state/zip) for regional sales analysis, shipping cost modeling, and fraud detection (address reuse). Also used for geocoding if needed.
business_questions: Which cities or states generate the most orders and revenue?
Are there address hotspots indicating repeat shipments to same physical address?
shipping_address.nl2sql_hints: Address is free text; consider pre-processing to extract city/state/zip into structured fields. For NL2SQL, support pattern searches (LIKE '%City%') or parsing rules to map to geography. Be cautious with inconsistent formatting and use trimming/standardization.

promotion_id.description: Identifier for applied promotion (nvarchar). Very sparse: only 3 distinct promotion ids and 94.5% nulls.
promotion_id.analysis_use: Attribute orders to promotions to measure promotion usage, incremental lift, and discount spend. Join to promotions table for promotion metadata (start/end, type).
business_questions: Which promotions generated the most orders and revenue?
What is the average discount per promotion and associated uplift in order volume?
promotion_id.nl2sql_hints: Join to promotions on exact string match. Treat nulls as 'no promotion'. When generating NL2SQL consider filters like WHERE promotion_id IS NOT NULL or promotion_id = 'PROMO0018'. Because sparsity is high, avoid joins without filtering to reduce processing.

delivery_date.description: Recorded delivery date (nvarchar). Distinct values ~997 and ~35% nulls (not yet delivered or not recorded).
delivery_date.analysis_use: Measure fulfillment performance (delivery lag = delivery_date - order_date), compute on-time delivery rates, and analyze delays by region or shipping method.
business_questions: What is the average time from order placement to delivery?
What share of deliveries are late relative to expected SLAs?
delivery_date.nl2sql_hints: Cast to DATE/DATETIME. Handle nulls (non-delivered/missing). When computing differences, ensure both order_date and delivery_date are parsed to same granularity and timezone. For NL2SQL, translate relative-date questions into date arithmetic (DATEDIFF) with appropriate casting.

product_id.description: Primary product identifier from the source system (string). Appears to be unique per row (distinct ~= row count).
product_id.analysis_use: Use for exact joins to order_items, inventory_transactions or suppliers (if they reference product_id). Grouping or selecting product-level metrics; deduplication and lineage checks.
business_questions: Which product_ids have the highest sales when joined to order_items?
Are there duplicate product_id entries or changes over time for the same product_id?
product_id.nl2sql_hints: Treat as a text key in joins. Do not cast to numeric. Prefer equality joins on product_id. When writing NL2SQL mappings, confirm whether downstream tables use product_id or sku. If product_id is nearly unique, use it for lookups; handle NULLs and trim whitespace before joining.

product_name.analysis_use: Filtering and fuzzy matching (LIKE, ILIKE) for product searches, grouping similar names, deduplication, and display in result sets. Useful for text-based analytics and keyword filters.
business_questions: Show products whose names contain 'ClassicReads' or 'TrendWear'.
Which product_name variants correspond to the same SKU or product_id (duplicate detection)?
product_name.nl2sql_hints: Use case-insensitive substring matching or full-text search where available. Expect minor variations; recommend fuzzy match or normalized comparisons (lower(trim(product_name))). Avoid exact equality for business queries that are user-typed. Limit returned name lengths in results and prefer top-n when matching by text.

category.description: High-level product category (e.g., Clothing, Electronics, Books).
category.analysis_use: Primary grouping dimension for reporting, aggregation, and filtering. Low cardinality makes it efficient for GROUP BY, roll-ups, and filters.
business_questions: What is the count of products per category?
Which categories have the highest average price or margin?
category.nl2sql_hints: Treat as categorical/string; use exact matches or IN lists for filters. Be mindful of NULLs and capitalization inconsistencies; normalize using lower() or UPPER() if needed. Good candidate for indexing or a lookup table in the NL2SQL ontology.

subcategory.description: More granular classification under category (e.g., Footwear, Accessories).
subcategory.analysis_use: Drill-down dimension under category for finer aggregations, inventory and assortment analyses, and targeted promotions.
business_questions: Within Clothing, which subcategories have the most SKUs?
Which subcategories have low average stock levels?
subcategory.nl2sql_hints: Use for grouping and hierarchical filters (category + subcategory). Normalize values for consistent matching. Consider mapping to a controlled vocabulary if user queries reference synonyms.

brand.description: Manufacturer or brand name associated with the product.
brand.analysis_use: Group and filter to analyze brand performance, SKU counts per brand, and supplier-brand relationships.
business_questions: Which brands contribute the most products to the catalog?
What is the average margin by brand?
brand.nl2sql_hints: Treat as a categorical field. Use exact match or IN for brand filters. Brands may have alternate namings; include synonyms/alias mapping in NL2SQL schema to improve accuracy (e.g., 'PerformMax' vs 'Perform Max').

sku.description: Stock keeping unit identifier (string). Typically unique per sellable item or variant.
sku.analysis_use: Join/lookup key for inventory and order tables, product-level filters, and SKU-level aggregations for stock or sales. Useful for operational queries (picking, fulfillment).
business_questions: Find SKU-level stock_quantity and supplier for SKU 'SKU-BEA-000427'.
Which SKUs have had price changes when compared to historical price data?
sku.nl2sql_hints: Treat as text key for joins. Verify whether downstream tables use sku or product_id; some systems use sku for inventory while product_id is master. Use equality joins; consider prefix/suffix patterns (SKU-*) for wildcard queries. Handle NULLs and trimming.

unit_price.description: Selling price per unit stored as nvarchar text (floating numeric values in string form).
unit_price.analysis_use: Must be cast to decimal/float for aggregations and arithmetic (revenue, average price, top priced products, price distributions). Used in margin and pricing analyses.
business_questions: What are the top 10 most expensive products by unit_price?
What is the average unit_price by category or brand?
unit_price.nl2sql_hints: CAST/CONVERT to DECIMAL/NUMERIC before SUM/AVG or comparisons. Be careful with string representations with long floating decimals. Handle non-numeric/NULL entries (use TRY_CAST/TRY_CONVERT or WHERE unit_price LIKE '%[^0-9.-]%' filters). Avoid equality comparisons on float values—use ranges or rounding for user queries.

cost_price.description: Cost to acquire/manufacture the product stored as nvarchar text (floating numeric values as strings).
cost_price.analysis_use: Cast to numeric for cost-based analyses: margin calculation, gross margin %, cost-of-goods-sold estimates, supplier cost comparisons.
business_questions: Which products have the lowest cost but high selling price (high margin)?
What is total cost value of current catalog stock (after joining to stock_quantity)?
cost_price.nl2sql_hints: Same handling as unit_price: convert to DECIMAL/NUMERIC, use TRY/CATCH style casts to handle malformed strings, coalesce NULLs where appropriate. When computing margin, join with unit_price after casts and guard divide-by-zero.

stock_quantity.description: Current on-hand inventory quantity snapshot, stored as nvarchar (integers represented as text).
stock_quantity.analysis_use: Cast to integer for inventory-level aggregations: total inventory value, low-stock alerts, reorder calculations, stock per supplier or category.
business_questions: List products with stock_quantity below 10 for reordering.
What is the total number of items in stock per supplier?
stock_quantity.nl2sql_hints: CAST to INTEGER/NUMERIC before comparisons. Watch for non-integer values or formatting. Use safe casts (TRY_CAST) and filter out NULLs. When joining to inventory_transactions, reconcile differences (transactions may be incremental). Because this is a snapshot, indicate the point-in-time in natural language queries.

supplier_id.description: Identifier of the supplier providing the product (string), can link to suppliers table.
supplier_id.analysis_use: Join key to suppliers table for procurement analytics, supplier-level product counts, lead-time and cost comparisons.
business_questions: Which supplier supplies the most products by count?
What is the average unit_price of goods from supplier SUP00079?
supplier_id.nl2sql_hints: Treat as text key in joins. Validate whether suppliers table uses the same format. Handle NULL or missing supplier_id when building supplier-level reports. Consider grouping by supplier_id and joining to a suppliers dimension to show names.

weight_kg.description: Product weight in kilograms stored as nvarchar text (floating numeric strings).
weight_kg.analysis_use: Cast to numeric to compute shipping cost estimates, weight-based grouping, packaging and logistics calculations, dimensional weight comparisons.
business_questions: Which products weigh more than 10 kg (affects shipping class)?
What is the total weight of current stock for transportation planning?
weight_kg.nl2sql_hints: CAST to DECIMAL/NUMERIC. Be aware of inconsistent precision/formatting and possible NULLs. Use ranges rather than exact float equality. If calculating totals multiply weight_kg * CAST(stock_quantity AS INT) and handle errors from malformed text.

created_date.description: Date the product was created/added to the catalog stored as text in ISO-like format (YYYY-MM-DD).
created_date.analysis_use: Cast to DATE/DATE/TIMESTAMP for time-series analyses such as product onboarding cadence, cohorting new products, and trend analysis by month/quarter/year.
business_questions: How many new products were added each month in the last year?
Which products were created after 2024-01-01?
created_date.nl2sql_hints: Use CAST/CONVERT to DATE (ISO format suggests safe conversion) or TRY_CAST for robustness. Normalize date formats if there are other variants. For NL2SQL, translate natural language time windows (e.g., 'last 6 months') to explicit date filters. Watch for timezones only if timestamps are added later; here values appear date-only.

promotion_id.description: Unique identifier for a promotion (catalog key).
promotion_id.analysis_use: Primary grouping/filter key for promotion-level analysis (counts, lookups, joins to orders/order_items). Candidate primary key - use for joins to redemption or audit tables.
business_questions: What promotion (promotion_id) generated the most redemptions?
Show the details for promotion PROMO0001.
promotion_id.nl2sql_hints: Treat as string key. It appears unique (distinct ~ row count). Use exact equality or IN lists for filters. If joining to other tables, ensure the other table stores the same format (no extra whitespace/case differences).

promotion_name.analysis_use: Display label in reports, groupings for readability, fuzzy matching for similar promotions. Not suitable as join key.
business_questions: List promotions and their names active in Q3 2024.
Find promotions whose name contains 'Bundle' or 'BOGO'.
promotion_name.nl2sql_hints: Use LIKE/ILIKE or full-text when matching parts of name. Beware of duplicate or similar names; prefer promotion_id for precise filters. Return name as display column in result sets.

promotion_type.description: Category of promotion logic (e.g., Bundle Deal, BOGO, Percentage Discount, Fixed Amount Off, Cashback).
promotion_type.analysis_use: Group and aggregate promotions by type; filter analytic logic (e.g., only percentage discounts should use discount_percentage). Useful for conditional calculations and validation.
business_questions: How many active percentage discounts do we have versus BOGO?
What is the distribution of promotion types across categories?
promotion_type.nl2sql_hints: Low-cardinality field — ideal for GROUP BY. Map type strings to expected numeric columns (e.g., 'Percentage Discount' -> use discount_percentage). Be careful about synonyms or casing differences; normalize prior to comparison.

discount_percentage.description: Percent-off value for percentage-type promotions (stored as text). Many rows null — only relevant for percentage discounts.
discount_percentage.analysis_use: Cast to numeric to compute summary statistics (mean, median, distribution), filter promotions with percent above/below thresholds, calculate applied discounts when joining to order amounts.
business_questions: What is the average discount_percentage for percentage-based promotions?
List percentage promotions with discount_percentage >= 25%.
discount_percentage.nl2sql_hints: Column stored as nvarchar; convert to DECIMAL/NUMERIC before numeric comparisons. Treat NULL as 'not applicable'. When parsing from NL, if user asks for '%', remove percent symbol or accept decimal strings. Only filter these when promotion_type = 'Percentage Discount' unless user explicitly requests otherwise.

discount_amount.description: Fixed discount amount (monetary) applied by some promotions (stored as text). Mostly NULL except for fixed-amount promotions or cashback entries.
discount_amount.analysis_use: Cast to numeric for aggregations and threshold filters; used when promotion_type indicates fixed amount or cashback. Useful to compute maximum possible discount value and compare to max_discount_amount.
business_questions: Which promotions offer a fixed discount_amount greater than 100?
Sum of discount_amount values across all currently active fixed-amount promotions.
discount_amount.nl2sql_hints: Convert to DECIMAL for arithmetic. Consider currency/units consistency (no explicit currency column). Only meaningful for promotion types that use a fixed amount; treat NULLs as not-applicable.

start_date.description: Promotion start date (stored as string YYYY-MM-DD).
start_date.analysis_use: Convert to DATE/TIMESTAMP for time-window queries, to compute durations, filter promotions that overlap a given date range, or to join with order timestamps to find applicable redemptions.
business_questions: Which promotions started in April 2023?
List promotions that start within the next 30 days.
start_date.nl2sql_hints: Cast to DATE. Accept common NL date expressions (e.g., 'last quarter', 'Q3 2024') and translate to date ranges. Handle inclusive/exclusive semantics (e.g., active on start_date). Validate malformed or null strings before casting.

end_date.description: Promotion end date (stored as string YYYY-MM-DD).
end_date.analysis_use: Cast to DATE for determining active promotions, computing durations and overlaps, and filtering promotions that expire within a timeframe.
business_questions: Which promotions expire this month?
Show promotions that ran longer than 90 days.
end_date.nl2sql_hints: Cast to DATE. When answering 'active' queries, compare end_date >= current_date (or inclusive as business requires). Handle NULL/blank as 'no end' or invalid—explicitly define behavior in NL2SQL mapping.

applicable_customer_type.description: Target customer segment for the promotion: B2B, B2C, or ALL.
applicable_customer_type.analysis_use: Filter or group promotions by target customer type; join logic to customer segments when joining to orders/customers to find eligible customers.
business_questions: How many promotions target B2B customers?
List promotions that are applicable to ALL customer types.
applicable_customer_type.nl2sql_hints: Small controlled vocabulary — match exact tokens (B2B, B2C, ALL). When mapping NL queries like 'business customers', normalize to 'B2B'. When joining to customers, ensure customer segmentation is aligned with these codes.

applicable_category.description: Product category that the promotion applies to (e.g., Beauty, Home & Kitchen). NULL means category unspecified or applies broadly.
applicable_category.analysis_use: Group promotions by category; join to products table on category to evaluate product-level impact and to find which products were eligible for a promo.
business_questions: Which categories have the most promotions?
List active promotions applicable to Electronics.
applicable_category.nl2sql_hints: Values may need normalization to match products.category (case, punctuation). Use equality or IN for multiple categories. If NULL means 'all categories', clarify semantics before filtering. Joins on category are fuzzy if product categories are hierarchical — consider normalization or mapping table.

min_order_amount.description: Minimum order total required to qualify for the promotion (stored as text).
min_order_amount.analysis_use: Cast to numeric for filtering promotions by threshold, to compute how many orders would have qualified given order totals (needs join), and to group promotions by entry threshold tiers.
business_questions: How many promotions require a min_order_amount of 100 or more?
Which promotions have no minimum order (min_order_amount = 0)?
min_order_amount.nl2sql_hints: Convert to numeric type before comparisons. Watch for string values like '0' vs NULL. Interpret NULL as 'no minimum' only if business semantics support it; otherwise treat as unknown.

max_discount_amount.description: Maximum discount cap applied to a promotion (monetary limit, stored as text). Many NULLs indicate no cap or unspecified.
max_discount_amount.analysis_use: Cast to numeric for aggregation and to compute effective maximum liability per usage; filter promos with caps below/above thresholds. Important when estimating maximum cost of a promotion.
business_questions: Which promotions have a max_discount_amount <= 100?
How many promotions have no max_discount_amount (NULL)?
max_discount_amount.nl2sql_hints: Convert to DECIMAL. Treat NULL as 'no cap' if that is the business meaning — clarify NL intent. Use for arithmetic only after casting; if comparing to order-level values, ensure currency units align.

status.analysis_use: Primary filter for operational views (show only Active promos), used to compute counts of active vs expired promotions, and for lifecycle reporting.
business_questions: List all Active promotions as of today.
How many promotions expired last quarter?
status.nl2sql_hints: Low-cardinality boolean-like field. Combine with start_date/end_date for robust 'active' semantics. Beware of stale status values — for queries based on date ranges prefer date-based filtering rather than status alone.

usage_limit_per_customer.description: Maximum number of times a single customer can use the promotion (stored as text). NULL indicates unlimited or unspecified.
usage_limit_per_customer.analysis_use: Cast to integer for analytics on promotion limits, to compute potential uptake per customer and to filter promotions with strict per-customer limits.
business_questions: Which promotions limit usage to 1 per customer?
List promotions with usage_limit_per_customer >= 3.
usage_limit_per_customer.nl2sql_hints: Convert to INT. Interpret NULL explicitly (unlimited vs missing). When translating NL like 'promotions limited to one use', map to usage_limit_per_customer = 1. Be mindful of decimals in stored strings (e.g., '5.0') — cast robustly (round/truncate) or normalize source.

total_usage_limit.description: Total number of times the promotion can be used across all customers (global cap, stored as text).
total_usage_limit.analysis_use: Cast to integer to estimate remaining capacity (when joined with redemption counts), to filter promotions by total availability, and to model maximum promotion liability.
business_questions: Which promotions have a total_usage_limit less than 200?
Show promotions with no total_usage_limit (unlimited).
total_usage_limit.nl2sql_hints: Convert to INT. Treat NULL as 'unlimited' or unknown based on business rules. Use alongside aggregated redemption counts (join to orders/order_items) to determine remaining uses.

store_id.description: Unique store identifier (string). Appears to uniquely identify rows; high cardinality with 2500 distinct values across 2500 rows.
store_id.analysis_use: Primary identifier for joining to transactional data (orders, inventory_transactions, order_items). Use for grouping by store and for selecting single-store details.
business_questions: What are the details (name, city, opening_date, size) for store STR000123?
Which store IDs have the highest employee_count or largest size?
store_id.nl2sql_hints: Treat as a categorical/text join key. Use exact matches for equality predicates. Watch for leading/trailing spaces or case differences. Recommend defining it as a primary key or at least unique index. Do not attempt numeric casts.

customer_id.description: Identifier for the customer/account associated with the store (string). Not unique at row level; 1502 distinct values.
customer_id.analysis_use: Join key to customers table for account-level rollups; grouping stores by owning customer; filter by customer to get associated stores.
business_questions: How many stores does customer CUST0000015 own?
List stores for a given customer for account management
customer_id.nl2sql_hints: Use as FK for joins to customers.customer_id. Treat as text; expect many-to-one (stores -> customer). Normalize whitespace and case before joins; consider index on this column for join performance.

store_name.description: Human-readable store name (string). Useful for display, fuzzy matching and grouping similar brand names.
store_name.analysis_use: Display attribute in results; grouping for brand/chain-level analysis if naming convention consistent (e.g., 'Global Shop - City'). Useful for approximate string matching and deduplication.
business_questions: Which stores have 'Global Shop' in their name and where are they located?
Show top store names by count (to find chains/brands)
store_name.nl2sql_hints: Often used in free-text user queries. Support LIKE and ILIKE (case-insensitive substring) matching and tokenization for NL2SQL. Consider pattern normalization (strip suffix like '-' or city tokens) when grouping by brand.

store_type.description: Categorical store channel/type (Modern Trade, Traditional Trade, Ecommerce). Low cardinality (3 values).
store_type.analysis_use: Important grouping/filtering dimension for channel analysis (compare metrics across channels). Good candidate for indexed categorical filters.
business_questions: How many stores are classified as 'Ecommerce' vs 'Modern Trade'?
List all Ecommerce stores in a given state
store_type.nl2sql_hints: Exact-match filtering is common. Map synonyms (e.g., 'online' -> 'Ecommerce') in NL2SQL layer. Because low cardinality, use equality or IN lists rather than full-text.

address.description: Street address for the store (string). High cardinality and largely unique.
address.analysis_use: Display/detailed lookup, geocoding for mapping, deduplication tasks. Less useful for aggregations but necessary for logistics and store visits.
business_questions: What is the full address for store STR000010?
Provide addresses for stores in ZIP code 21473
address.nl2sql_hints: Use as display or exact/LIKE filter. May contain inconsistent formatting — consider normalization for matching. Avoid using address as a join key.

city.description: City where the store is located (string). Medium-low cardinality (18 distinct).
city.analysis_use: Common grouping/filtering dimension for regional reporting and mapping. Useful for aggregation of store counts and joining to city-level demographic/enrichment data.
business_questions: How many stores are in Houston vs New York?
Which stores in Seattle are Ecommerce?
