city.nl2sql_hints: Use equality/IN filters. Be careful with synonyms or alternate spellings (abbreviations). Good candidate for pre-populated autocomplete in NL2SQL interfaces.

state.description: Two-letter state code (string). 11 distinct values in dataset; common grouping dimension.
state.analysis_use: Geographic rollups at state level, regional filters, policy/region-based analyses.
business_questions: Show number of stores by state and store_type
List stores in state 'IL' that opened after 2024-01-01
state.nl2sql_hints: Treat as standardized code; use equality and IN. Normalize input (upper-case). Often combined with city for disambiguation. Indexing beneficial for regional queries.

country.description: Country code or name (string). In this dataset contains only 'USA'.
country.analysis_use: Typically a top-level filter or left out when single-valued. Useful if dataset expands internationally.
business_questions: How many stores are in the USA?
Filter stores by country when dataset contains multiple countries
country.nl2sql_hints: Often constant; NL2SQL can treat as optional. If single-valued, omit from default filters; when multi-country, ensure consistent codes (ISO).

postal_code.description: Postal / ZIP code for the store (string). High cardinality (2464 distinct).
postal_code.analysis_use: Geographic granularity below city; useful for delivery radius calculations, mapping, and grouping by postal areas. Good for joins to ZIP-level external datasets.
business_questions: List stores in ZIP code 27454
How many stores fall within a set of postal codes for a campaign?
postal_code.nl2sql_hints: Treat as string (can contain leading zeros). Use equality or IN. Avoid numeric casts that remove leading zeros. Normalize whitespace. Consider using prefix matching for ZIP ranges.

opening_date.description: Store opening date, currently stored as nvarchar (appears ISO yyyy-mm-dd). 766 distinct values and many recent dates in sample.
opening_date.analysis_use: Cohorting and time-based analyses (age of store, new stores within period). Must cast to DATE or TIMESTAMP for proper comparisons, date_trunc and range filters.
business_questions: Which stores opened in the last year?
Count of stores opened per month in 2025
opening_date.nl2sql_hints: Convert to DATE in queries (CAST/opening_date::date) and validate format; handle nulls and malformed values. NL2SQL must recognize natural language date expressions and map to date comparisons after casting.

size_sqft.description: Store size in square feet, stored as nvarchar. High cardinality and numeric in meaning.
size_sqft.analysis_use: Aggregation (avg, median, sum), grouping by size buckets (small/medium/large), store footprint analysis. Must cast to numeric for arithmetic operations.
business_questions: What is the average size_sqft by store_type?
List top 10 largest stores by size_sqft
size_sqft.nl2sql_hints: Cast to integer/decimal (e.g., CAST(size_sqft AS INT)) with safe handling of non-numeric values. Consider precomputing numeric column for performance. Use numeric comparisons in filters (>, <) after casting.

employee_count.description: Number of employees at the store, stored as nvarchar. 196 distinct values; numeric in meaning.
employee_count.analysis_use: Aggregation (sum, avg), productivity metrics when combined with sales, staff planning and headcount analyses. Requires casting to numeric.
business_questions: Which stores have more than 150 employees?
Average employee_count by city or store_type
employee_count.nl2sql_hints: Cast to INT for numeric comparisons and aggregations. Handle nulls and possible textual entries. NL2SQL parsers should map natural language 'stores with > 100 employees' to a numeric cast and predicate.

supplier_id.description: Unique identifier for the supplier (nvarchar). Appears unique across rows but stored as text and can be NULL.
supplier_id.analysis_use: Primary join key candidate for linking suppliers to products, orders, inventory and order_items; used for grouping and exact-match filters to retrieve supplier records; can be used to deduplicate or identify missing supplier references in transactional tables.
business_questions: Which supplier (by supplier_id) supplies a given product or has the most orders?
Are there any supplier_id values referenced in other tables that do not exist in this table?
supplier_id.nl2sql_hints: Treat as a string key — use exact equality for filters (e.g., supplier_id = 'SUP00001') and trim/case normalize when joining. Because it is nullable and not enforced as a PK, query generators should include existence checks (IS NOT NULL) and consider deduplication (SELECT DISTINCT) when necessary. Avoid numeric casts; preserve leading zeros and literal formatting.

supplier_name.description: Human-readable supplier company name (nvarchar). Not strictly unique; some repeated or similar names exist.
supplier_name.analysis_use: Use for display, fuzzy matching, grouping when supplier_id is unavailable, and text searches. Useful for reporting and human-facing queries; can be used to aggregate by supplier brand or family.
business_questions: Show details for 'Premier Components Co.' including contact and rating.
Which supplier names appear most frequently or have multiple supplier_ids (possible duplicates)?
supplier_name.nl2sql_hints: Text matching should support case-insensitive comparisons and LIKE or ILIKE for partial matches (e.g., '%Premier%'). When joining, prefer supplier_id; only use supplier_name for joins if normalization and deduplication are applied. Be cautious of similar names — consider fuzzy match logic outside simple SQL if needed.

contact_person.description: Primary contact person at the supplier (nvarchar), typically a name or role.
contact_person.analysis_use: Used for contact completeness checks, reporting contact points per supplier and routing communications. Can be used as a display field in results and for filters searching specific contacts.
business_questions: Which suppliers have a listed contact person and which do not?
Who is the contact person for supplier SUP00010?
contact_person.nl2sql_hints: Treat as free-text. Use IS NULL / IS NOT NULL to check presence and LIKE for partial name matches. Avoid using as a join key. NL2SQL should map natural language references to exact or partial string matches (e.g., 'contact person named "John"').

email.description: Supplier contact email address (nvarchar), used for electronic communications.
email.analysis_use: Validate contact completeness, filter suppliers by domain (e.g., '@premiercomponents.com'), and extract domain statistics for vendor classification. Useful for identifying suppliers reachable by email.
business_questions: How many suppliers have corporate email addresses vs generic ones?
List suppliers with missing or malformed email addresses.
email.nl2sql_hints: Contains special characters; use LIKE '%@domain%' to filter by domain. Validate format with simple patterns (CHARINDEX('@', email) > 0) or regex if supported. Use IS NULL/='' to test missing entries. NL2SQL should not assume uniqueness; exact match filters must be quoted.

phone.description: Supplier contact phone number (nvarchar), may include country codes and punctuation.
phone.analysis_use: Completeness checks, grouping by country code, or extracting region-based contact patterns. Display field for human users and filter for suppliers reachable by phone.
business_questions: Which suppliers do not have a phone number on file?
How many suppliers use a +1 country code vs other country codes?
phone.nl2sql_hints: Phone numbers are free-form; use LIKE for pattern matching (e.g., phone LIKE '+1-%') and string functions to extract country codes. Normalize formatting before joining or grouping if strict comparisons are required. Handle NULLs and empty strings consistently.

country.description: Country of the supplier (nvarchar); low cardinality with top entries like China, France, Italy, Japan, South Korea.
country.analysis_use: Key attribute for geographical segmentation, grouping, aggregation (counts, average rating by country) and filtering (e.g., suppliers in Asia or Europe). Useful when joining to region tables or mapping supplier locations.
business_questions: How are suppliers distributed across countries and regions?
Which country has the highest average supplier rating?
country.nl2sql_hints: Text values with low cardinality — use equality or IN lists (country IN ('China','Japan')). NL2SQL should allow region mappings (e.g., Asia) via additional logic or mapping tables. Consider trimming and case-normalization for comparisons. Indexing country can speed group/aggregation queries on larger datasets.

rating.description: Supplier quality/performance rating stored as nvarchar; values look numeric (decimals) but are text (e.g., '3.10000000000000', '4.0').
rating.analysis_use: Key metric for supplier performance analysis: compute averages, min/max, distribution, and filter top/bottom suppliers. Requires casting to numeric for accurate aggregations and comparisons.
business_questions: Which suppliers have a rating above 4.0?
What is the average supplier rating overall and by country?
rating.nl2sql_hints: Must CAST/CONVERT to numeric (DECIMAL/NUMERIC or FLOAT) before aggregating or comparing (e.g., CAST(rating AS decimal(5,2))). Watch for malformed or NULL values — include safe-casting or filtering (WHERE TRY_CAST(rating AS decimal(5,2)) IS NOT NULL). NL2SQL should not assume integer semantics; preserve decimal precision and consider rounding for display.

established_year.description: Year the supplier was established stored as nvarchar; values appear as 4-digit years (e.g., '1986').
established_year.analysis_use: Compute supplier age cohorts, filter by year ranges (established before/after a given year), and segment long-standing vs newer suppliers. Requires casting to integer for numeric comparisons and sorting.
business_questions: List suppliers established before 1990.
How many suppliers were established in each decade?
established_year.nl2sql_hints: CAST/CONVERT to integer for numeric comparisons and range filters (e.g., CAST(established_year AS int)). Guard against non-year or NULL values (use TRY_CAST where available). When grouping by decade, derive decade via arithmetic on the integer year (e.g., (CAST(established_year AS int)/10)*10). Normalize whitespace before casting.
